Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty scattered. I tried to carve out time in the evenings after work, usually around 9 or 10 PM when things quieted down. I'd put on my headphones and listen to lectures while taking notes, since I absorb information better when I hear it. Some weeks I managed to stick to a schedule, but other weeks life just got in the way and I had to squeeze studying in wherever I could find a spare hour.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was due. When deadlines were approaching, I'd cram in extra hours wherever possible, sometimes staying up late or waking up early. During quieter weeks, I tried to maintain some consistency, maybe studying three or four evenings a week. But with limited resources and other responsibilities, I couldn't always stick to an ideal schedule even when I wanted to.
Q3: Usually it was either checking if new materials had been posted or seeing if there were any announcements about assignments. Sometimes I'd log in just to replay a lecture video because I hadn't fully understood something the first time. Deadlines were the biggest motivator though—I'd get anxious about missing something important, so I'd check in to make sure I was on track.
Q4: It took a few weeks, but eventually logging into the LMS became something I did almost automatically in the evenings. I'd finish dinner, make some tea, and open up the platform to see what needed my attention. The peer support group I was connected with helped because we'd sometimes coordinate through the platform, which gave me another reason to check in regularly. It wasn't perfect, but it became a habit of sorts.
Q5: The lecture recordings were absolutely essential for me since I'm an auditory learner. Being able to pause, rewind, and re-listen to explanations made a huge difference in my understanding. The discussion forums were also helpful when other students asked questions I hadn't thought of—reading those exchanges sometimes clarified concepts better than the original materials did.
Q6: I'll admit I barely touched the reading materials and PDFs. I know they were probably important, but reading dense text on a screen after a long day just didn't work for me. I'd download them with good intentions but rarely got through them. The optional practice quizzes also got ignored because I was always scrambling to complete the required work first.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could immediately see how it connected to what I needed to know for assignments or exams. If I could listen to something and walk away feeling like I understood a concept better, that felt valuable. Activities that seemed disconnected from the main assessments or took too long without clear payoff just felt like luxuries I couldn't afford.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a nice-to-have that I'd get to 'someday'—except someday never came. Required activities had this urgency attached to them that made me prioritize them no matter what. With optional stuff, even when I knew it might help, there was always something more pressing demanding my attention. It's frustrating because I think I missed out on things that could have helped me.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded almost all the audio lectures to my phone so I could listen to them during my commute or while doing chores. I also had a notebook where I wrote out key concepts and reviewed those notes constantly. Sometimes I'd find YouTube videos that explained topics differently, which helped when the course materials weren't clicking for me.
Q10: There were many times when I was actively studying but the LMS wouldn't show it. Like when I'd listen to downloaded lectures on the bus, or when I'd spend an hour reviewing my handwritten notes before bed. I also studied with a peer from my support group in person a few times, going over concepts together. None of that showed up anywhere online.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my main approach. I'd stream a video once to make sure it downloaded properly, then I'd watch it multiple times offline over the following days. My internet at home isn't always reliable, so downloading everything first thing was essential. The LMS probably showed me watching something for thirty minutes when really I spent hours with that same content.
Q12: Around midterms, everything hit at once and I really struggled. I had extra shifts at work that I couldn't turn down because I needed the money, and the course material was getting more complex. There was a week where I barely logged in at all—I was exhausted and overwhelmed. I kept telling myself I'd catch up, but falling behind just made everything feel heavier.
Q13: Definitely. When I was tired or stressed from work, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. Sometimes I'd click through materials just to feel like I was doing something, but my brain wasn't engaged. Competing responsibilities were constant—I don't have the luxury of being a full-time student, so the course always had to fit around everything else.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd have a lecture playing but realize I'd zoned out and missed the last ten minutes. Or I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just going through the motions. It's frustrating because I was physically present with the materials but mentally somewhere else entirely, worrying about bills or work or just being too tired to focus.
Q15: I postponed the longer readings constantly, even though I knew they'd help with assignments. There was always this voice saying I'd do it tomorrow when I had more energy. Some of the more challenging problem sets got pushed back too because I was afraid of struggling with them and feeling stupid. The avoidance made things worse in the end, but in the moment it felt like self-preservation.
Q16: Deadlines completely controlled my LMS usage. In the days before something was due, I'd be on the platform constantly, frantically reviewing materials and trying to piece together my assignments. After a deadline passed, I'd almost disappear for a few days to recover. It wasn't healthy, but that pressure was often the only thing that could cut through my exhaustion and competing responsibilities.
Q17: Feedback was honestly hit or miss for me. When I got encouraging comments, it motivated me to engage more with the next module. But when feedback was critical or I scored poorly, I'd feel deflated and avoid the platform for a bit. The peer support helped here—talking to others who were also struggling made the negative feedback feel less personal and more like something I could work through.
Q18: The course structure was pretty rigid, which had pros and cons. On one hand, having clear deadlines and a set sequence of materials gave me something to follow. On the other hand, there wasn't much flexibility for someone like me who sometimes needed to pause and catch up. The pacing assumed everyone could dedicate the same amount of time each week, which just wasn't my reality.
Q19: Having video lectures made engagement much easier for me as an auditory learner—I could absorb information while doing other things. But the heavy reliance on reading materials made things harder. Also, discussion posts requiring original contributions felt intimidating; I mostly just read what others wrote rather than posting myself. A bit more flexibility in how we could demonstrate engagement would have helped.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is genuinely trying but struggling to produce quality work. They might be reviewing materials repeatedly, trying to understand, but when it comes to assignments, they freeze up or run out of time. It could also be someone dealing with anxiety or perfectionism—they're engaging with content but can't bring themselves to submit imperfect work. I've definitely felt that way.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. Some of my best understanding came from offline studying that never showed up in the LMS. If someone has good materials downloaded, strong external resources, or study groups outside the platform, their low activity might not reflect their actual learning. The LMS is just one window into what students are doing, and it's a pretty narrow one.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—like when someone logs in, how long they spend on videos, whether they complete tasks. That's useful for spotting someone who's completely disengaged. But logs miss the quality of engagement, whether someone is actually understanding or just clicking through. They miss offline studying, emotional state, and all the life circumstances that affect how someone shows up to their learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions to get a grade. It's when I'm curious about a topic and want to dig deeper, when I'm thinking about course concepts even outside of study time. For me, it's also about feeling connected—to the material, to peers, to some sense of purpose. When I'm truly engaged, learning doesn't feel like a chore.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I was trying despite everything working against me. It wouldn't show the hours I spent listening to lectures on the bus, the notes I rewrote by hand, the conversations with peers that helped me process difficult concepts. It wouldn't show that I genuinely wanted to succeed even when my exam scores suggested otherwise. My effort and my circumstances are invisible in those numbers.
